stories.title: AllianceBernstein Lends $75M on Master-Planned Rechler Industrial Park
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets has arranged a $75- million development facility for Rechler Business District, a master-planned industrial business park in Medford, NY, within Long Island’s Suffolk County. Senior managing director Peter Rotchford and managing director Tyler Peck worked on behalf of Rechler Equity Partners to secure the financing through AllianceBernstein L.P. Rechler Business District encompasses a newly constructed and fully leased 140,875-square-foot Class A shallow bay logistics facility at 10 Donald’s Way, 9.8 acres of fully leased industrial outdoor storage space at 25 Donald’s Way and approximately 45 acres of additional development-ready land. “Securing financing for a project of this scale required a lender that understood both the strength of the existing cash flow and the long-term value creation embedded in the to-be developed parcels,” said Rotchford. “AllianceBernstein recognized the compelling combination of stable income from an investment-grade tenant, a low leverage profile and Rechler’s track record for industrial development, which ultimately made this financing opportunity particularly attractive.” The post AllianceBernstein Lends $75M on Master-Planned Rechler Industrial Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: AllianceBernstein lends $75M to Rechler for industrial park in Medford, NY (Long Island/Suffolk County).
Asset includes 140,875 SF fully leased Class A logistics facility plus 45 acres development-ready land.
Low leverage, investment-grade tenant cash flow supports financing for future industrial expansion pipeline.
stories.summary: AllianceBernstein has provided a $75 million development facility to Rechler Business District, a master-planned industrial park in Medford, NY (Suffolk County). The deal finances a newly constructed, fully leased 140,875-square-foot Class A shallow-bay logistics facility, 9.8 acres of leased industrial outdoor storage, and approximately 45 acres of undeveloped land. The lender was attracted to stable cash flow from an investment-grade tenant and Rechler's track record, lending at a low leverage ratio.
stories.impact: Industrial development financing strength in Long Island/Suffolk County signals sustained logistics demand and capital availability for speculative land plays. This funds future supply in a market with limited industrial vacancy, potentially constraining multifamily developer access to labor and materials if construction clusters intensify. Monitor Long Island wage/labor inflation spillover into adjacent residential markets.

stories.title: Kinect Real Estate Partners Closes $127M Fund for MF Projects
stories.excerpt: Kinect Real Estate Partners, a Bellevue investment firm linked to multifamily developer American Capital Group, announced the $126.5 million close of Kinect Opportunity Fund II, the firm’s second GP fund, exceeding its $100 million target. The fund will focus on development and value-add opportunities in multifamily assets, with a development pipeline totaling approximately $1.6 billion and more than 3,000 apartment units across high-growth western markets including Bellevue, Redmond and Bothell in Washington, as well as Mountain View and San Diego in California. “We set out to extend a time-tested real estate strategy to the private wealth channel through a platform where operational depth and investment discipline are fully aligned,” said BJ Kuula, Co-Founder and Managing Partner of Kinect Real Estate Partners and CEO of American Capital Group. “The close of Kinect Opportunity Fund II reflects the momentum we’re seeing from investors who value that alignment and are increasingly seeking institutional-quality real estate opportunities backed by experienced operators.” The post Kinect Real Estate Partners Closes $127M Fund for MF Projects appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Kinect Real Estate Partners closes $126.5M Fund II (exceeded $100M target) for multifamily development.
Pipeline of $1.6B across 3,000+ units in Seattle (Bellevue, Redmond, Bothell) and San Francisco Bay Area (Mountain View) and San Diego.
Fund targets development and value-add in high-growth western markets; aligned platform combining operator expertise with institutional capital.
stories.summary: Kinect Real Estate Partners, linked to developer American Capital Group, has closed its second opportunity fund at $126.5 million, exceeding its $100 million target. The fund has a $1.6 billion development pipeline spanning more than 3,000 apartment units across high-growth western markets including the Seattle metro (Bellevue, Redmond, Bothell), San Francisco Bay Area (Mountain View), and San Diego. The fund targets development and value-add multifamily opportunities with operational alignment between capital and execution.
stories.impact: Significant institutional capital commitments to western multifamily development—particularly Seattle and Bay Area—signal investor confidence and likely accelerate competitive pipeline pressure in those tight markets. For TCR: watch for density of competing starts in Bellevue/Redmond (Amazon/tech hub effects) and Mountain View (Google proximity); early entitlements critical. Capital availability suggests cost-per-unit trending upward; underwriting discipline will diverge winners from marginal deals.

stories.title: Hyatt Regency to Take Over Vacant Tucson Hotel Tower
stories.excerpt: Hyatt Hotels Corporation, in collaboration with HSL Properties and Desert Hospitality Management, will debut the Hyatt Regency Tucson Convention Center, expected to open in late 2027. The hotel will mark the first Hyatt Regency-branded property in Tucson. The building, once known at the Hotel Arizona, has been vacant since 2012. Located in the heart of downtown Tucson and steps from the Tucson Convention Center, Linda Ronstadt Music Hall, Leo Rich Theater and Tucson Arena, Hyatt Regency Tucson Convention Center will become a destination for meetings, conventions, business travelers and leisure guests in the city’s vibrant urban core. The hotel’s extensive multi-million-dollar transformation will introduce 291 guestrooms and suites, a fitness center, an elevated pool experience and approximately 22,000 square feet of flexible meeting and event space. The property will feature a large ballroom and multiple breakout meeting spaces. The post Hyatt Regency to Take Over Vacant Tucson Hotel Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hyatt Regency takes over vacant Hotel Arizona in downtown Tucson; opens late 2027.
Building vacant since 2012; includes 291 guestrooms, 22,000 SF meeting space in convention district.
Multi-million-dollar renovation targets meetings, conventions, business travel in downtown urban core.
stories.summary: Hyatt Hotels, in partnership with HSL Properties and Desert Hospitality Management, will convert the long-vacant Hotel Arizona in downtown Tucson into the Hyatt Regency Tucson Convention Center, opening in late 2027. The 291-room, 22,000-square-foot meeting-space property will anchor Tucson's convention district near the Tucson Convention Center, Linda Ronstadt Music Hall, and other cultural venues. The property has been empty since 2012.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance; hospitality conversion does not generate apartment demand. However, downtown revitalization in Tucson may indirectly support foot traffic and mixed-use activation that could enhance rental appeal nearby. Monitor whether convention district upside translates into residential infill demand in downtown Tucson submarket.

stories.title: Citigroup Refis East Flatbush Apartments With $35M Loan
stories.excerpt: Developer Jacob Rosenberg has landed a $35 million loan to refinance a newly built multifamily property in Brooklyn’s East Flatbush neighborhood. Citigroup supplied the loan for the 95-unit apartment building at 406 Remsen Avenue that was completed in 2024. PincusCo. first reported the refi, which closed June 26. Meridian Capital Group negotiated the debt with a team led by Naphtali Marrus and Ben Nevid. “406 Remsen Avenue is a standout new addition to the Brooklyn rental market, and the sponsorship brought a clear vision for the asset’s next chapter,” Marrus said in a statement. In a statement, Nevid credited Citigroup with taking “the time to understand the asset and the sponsorship” to “structure a financing solution that met the borrower’s objectives.” Rosenberg, who owns 406 Remsen Avenue with Michael Rosenberg, acquired the 111,027-square-foot property near the Saratoga Avenue subway station for $2 million in late 2024, according to PincosCo. Community amenities include a fitness center, a rooftop terrace, garage parking and bike storage. Citigroup and Rosenberg did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Citigroup refi: $35M loan on 95-unit new construction at 406 Remsen Avenue, Brooklyn East Flatbush.
Building completed 2024; Meridian Capital Group arranged with Naphtali Marrus and Ben Nevid.
Submarket benefit from Saratoga Avenue subway access; developer acquired land for $2M in late 2024.
stories.summary: Developer Jacob Rosenberg secured a $35 million refinance from Citigroup for 406 Remsen Avenue, a newly completed 95-unit luxury apartment building in Brooklyn's East Flatbush neighborhood near the Saratoga Avenue subway station. The 111,027-square-foot property was built in 2024. Meridian Capital Group negotiated the transaction, which closed June 26. Rosenberg and Michael Rosenberg acquired the site for just $2 million in late 2024, indicating significant value creation through development.
stories.impact: East Flatbush rental market is attracting both development and aggressive refi capital at favorable terms shortly after delivery—signaling strong leasing velocity, stabilization, and lender appetite for transit-proximate Brooklyn product. This validates infill development strategy in underused subway-adjacent neighborhoods. For TCR: East Flatbush density and transit-oriented development may indicate favorable basis and rent growth trajectory; competitive pressure increasing as lenders de-risk completed assets.

stories.title: Lennar Closes on Former Office Building Site at Bishop Ranch Mixed-Use
stories.excerpt: Sunset Development has selected Lennar to build within Canopy, a 27-acre residential neighborhood that is being developed on the BR8 office building site at Bishop Ranch in San Ramon. Lennar closed on 25 acres within the larger Canopy parcel. Sunset Development retains ownership of the remaining two acres, designated for future affordable multi-housing. Demolition of the existing BR8 building and construction are expected in mid-to-late 2027. “Building the best suburban downtown in America means getting the residential experience right,” said Alex Mehran Jr., president and CEO of Sunset Development Company. “Lennar brings the experience and scale to deliver that, and we look forward to seeing Canopy become a vital part of what Bishop Ranch is becoming.” Within Canopy, Lennar will build 255 detached single-family homes ranging from two to three stories. The site transitions from three-story homes on the western edge to two-story homes abutting Summerhill’s City Village to the east. The post Lennar Closes on Former Office Building Site at Bishop Ranch Mixed-Use appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Lennar closes on 25 acres within 27-acre Canopy residential at Bishop Ranch, San Ramon (Bay Area).
Lennar to build 255 detached single-family homes on office demolition site (BR8); construction mid-to-late 2027.
2 acres retained for affordable multi-housing; office-to-residential conversion on major Bay Area master-planned community.
stories.summary: Sunset Development has selected Lennar to build Canopy, a 27-acre residential neighborhood on the former BR8 office building site within the larger Bishop Ranch mixed-use development in San Ramon, California. Lennar closed on 25 acres and will construct 255 detached single-family homes (two to three stories). Sunset Development retains 2 acres for future affordable multi-housing. Demolition and construction are expected to begin in mid-to-late 2027.
stories.impact: Office-to-residential conversion at scale (27 acres) in Bay Area master-planned community signals sustained macro demand for housing over office and lender/investor appetite for suburban residential infill. For TCR: watch Bishop Ranch for multifamily infill opportunities; Lennar's single-family focus may leave gap for mid-rise rental product. Transit access and affordability overlay critical to Bay Area underwriting. Monitor whether SF Bay area office conversions accelerate, creating additional land supply and submarket competition.

stories.title: U.S. Industrial Leasing, Absorption Rise More Than 25% Year-Over-Year
stories.excerpt: U.S. industrial leasing volume for the first half of 2026 increased 27.1% year-over-year to 490.6 million square feet, marking the third-strongest H1 for the sector after 2021 and 2022, while net absorption was up 28% from the year-ago period, Savills reported Monday. The firm’s preliminary Q2 report on U.S. industrial found that large-format leasing, particularly deals above 750,000 square feet, has been a key driver of the pickup. Although vacancy rose 10 basis points Y-O-Y, it held flat in the second quarter from the prior quarter, ending Q2 2026 at 8.2%, a further sign of stabilization at the midpoint of 2026. The construction pipeline totaled 320 million square feet in Q2 2026, well below the 782.4 million square feet of late 2022. Meanwhile, asking rents have been stable, rising 1.8% Y-O-Y. With vacancy still elevated and sublease availability ticking up, near-term rent growth is expected to remain modest, reported Savills. Pictured: International Commerce Center in Adairsville, GA, where Pactra signed a full-building lease for nearly 700,000 square feet in Q2. Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post U.S. Industrial Leasing, Absorption Rise More Than 25% Year-Over-Year appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: U.S. industrial leasing up 27.1% YoY in H1 2026 to 490.6M SF; net absorption up 28% YoY.
Large-format deals (750K+ SF) driving growth; construction pipeline at 320M SF (well below 782M in late 2022).
Vacancy flat Q2 2026 at 8.2%; rents up 1.8% YoY; near-term rent growth expected modest with elevated vacancy.
stories.summary: U.S. industrial leasing volume surged 27.1% year-over-year in the first half of 2026 to 490.6 million square feet, marking the third-strongest H1 on record (after 2021 and 2022), while net absorption climbed 28% year-over-year. Large-format deals above 750,000 square feet have been the key driver. The active construction pipeline totaled 320 million square feet in Q2 2026—well below the 782.4 million square feet in late 2022—indicating a supply correction. Vacancy held flat sequentially at 8.2%, and asking rents rose 1.8% year-over-year.
stories.impact: Industrial supply normalization (pipeline down 59% from late 2022 peak) will tighten logistics market regionally over 12–24 months, driving construction labor scarcity and wage pressure across key multifamily markets (Dallas, Atlanta, Phoenix, LA). Large-format lease momentum signals sustained e-commerce/3PL demand, supporting industrial wage spillovers into nearby residential submarkets. TCR should expect labor cost inflation acceleration by late 2026–2027 in industrial-heavy metros; early stage locking subcontractor rates critical.

stories.title: Affinius Capital Supplies $180M for Jersey Center Multifamily Tower
stories.excerpt: The Jersey City building boom continues unabated, this time in the growing Journal Square submarket. Namdar Group — one of the city’s most prolific developers in recent years — has secured $180 million to refinance 35 Cottage Street, a 27-story, 564-unit luxury apartment tower in Journal Square, Commercial Observer can first report. Affinius Capital provided the debt, structured as a floating-rate, interest-only bridge loan, while the Walker & Dunlop capital markets team of Aaron Appel, Jonathan Schwartz, Adam Schwartz, Keith Kurland, Dustin Stolly, Sean Reimer, Jordan Casella, Cole Grims and Edward Leboyer arranged the transaction. Schwartz, in a statement, called Journal Square one of the New York area’s “most dynamic mixed-use neighborhoods,” and said the submarket has continued to attract attention from the investment community. “35 Cottage Street is exceptionally well positioned to capitalize on that momentum through its transit-oriented location, differentiated product offering, and institutional-quality execution,” Schwartz added. Located at 35 Cottage Street — a short walk from the Journal Square PATH station — the new apartment tower has not yet opened, but will feature units ranging from studios to three-bedrooms. The initial plan for the tower included using three ground floors as a Chabad synagogue, a preschool and a party hall, as CO reported in August 2024. Namdar Group has been responsible for a prolific amount of new development in Jersey City. Last year, the firm secured a $335 million debt package to refinance three newly built Jersey City multifamily assets and a total of 833 units at 9 Homestead Place, 26 Van Reipen Avenue and 28 Cottage Street, CO reported. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Affinius Capital provides $180M floating-rate bridge to refinance 35 Cottage Street, Jersey City (564 units, 27 stories).
Namdar Group developed luxury tower in Journal Square submarket; Walker & Dunlop arranged transaction.
Journal Square noted as dynamic mixed-use neighborhood attracting investor attention post-delivery.
stories.summary: Namdar Group has secured $180 million in refinancing from Affinius Capital for 35 Cottage Street, a 27-story, 564-unit luxury apartment tower in Jersey City's Journal Square submarket. The debt was structured as a floating-rate, interest-only bridge loan. Walker & Dunlop's capital markets team arranged the transaction. Journal Square is characterized as one of the New York area's most dynamic mixed-use neighborhoods with ongoing investor momentum.
stories.impact: Bridge financing on luxury rental tower in Journal Square at post-completion stage signals rapid value-add and stabilization capture; floating-rate structures imply developer expects rate environment to soften or refinance into permanent fixed debt soon. Jersey City/Journal Square supply continues to densify; monitor competing deliveries (NJ Transit/PATH access, gentrification pace) for rent compression risk. For TCR: Journal Square market tightening; earlier stage entitlements and lower-cost gaps relative to downtown Jersey City may still offer returns.

stories.title: Waste Management Firm Buys Miami-Dade Trucking Terminal for $51M
stories.excerpt: Centerpoint has sold another trucking parking lot in Miami-Dade County, this time for $51 million to a waste management company, property records show. Woodlands, Texas-based Waste Connections purchased the 12-acre site at 17707 Northwest Miami Court in Miami Gardens, Fla. The surface parking lot — facing Interstate 95, just north of the intersection with Florida’s Turnpike — holds 370 spots and a 3,786-square-foot building. The buyer specializes in waste collection services for residential and commercial buildings. CenterPoint purchased the property for $47.5 million in 2022, according to property records. The recent transaction marks CenterPoint’s second sale of a trucking terminal in Miami-Dade County over the past year. In October, the Oak Brook, Ill.-based firm sold a 17-acre site near Miami International Airport for $52 million to trucking logistics company Outpost. CenterPoint had purchased the property for $29.5 million in 2020, resulting in a 77 percent increase in value over five years. Representatives for Waste Connections and CenterPoint did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Waste Connections buys 12-acre Miami-Dade trucking terminal for $51M at 17707 NW Miami Court, Miami Gardens.
CenterPoint Properties sold (purchased 2022 for $47.5M); second Miami-Dade logistics property sale in 1 year.
Industrial logistics consolidation and value capture in Miami-Dade; positive for CenterPoint exit strategy.
stories.summary: Waste Connections purchased a 12-acre trucking parking lot and terminal at 17707 Northwest Miami Court in Miami Gardens for $51 million. The surface parking facility holds 370 spaces and a 3,786-square-foot building. CenterPoint Properties sold the asset, which it had acquired for $47.5 million in 2022. This marks CenterPoint's second Miami-Dade logistics terminal sale in the past year (prior sale near Miami International Airport for $52 million).
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. Industrial logistics market in Miami-Dade remains active and profitable; CenterPoint's exit at modest gain suggests stable industrial asset values. Monitor whether industrial consolidation and supply-chain investment create wage pressures or land scarcity in Miami-Dade, potentially affecting multifamily labor/land costs in the metro.

stories.title: Avery Hall plots 250 units at stalled Crown Heights hotel site
stories.excerpt: One year on from purchasing the site out of bankruptcy, Avery Hall Investments is revealing the future for a Crown Heights lot once envisioned for a hotel. The Brooklyn-based developer filed an application with the New York City Department of Buildings for a mixed-use project on a vacant parcel at 1550 Bedford Avenue, the Commercial Observer reported. Through its land use application, Avery Hall is petitioning for zoning amendments to permit a mix of residential and commercial use, along with a mandatory inclusionary housing area designation for affordable housing. Avery Hall plans to build a 173,000-square-foot building with 250 units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Avery Hall filed application for 250-unit mixed-use at 1550 Bedford Ave, Crown Heights, Brooklyn (18 stories).
Site purchased out of bankruptcy for $10.1M; 75 units designated affordable; 173K SF includes retail.
Zoning amendment required; prior owner (Yoel Goldman) pursued hotel, folded during pandemic; liens exceeded $1.4M.
stories.summary: Avery Hall Investments has filed a New York City Department of Buildings application for a 250-unit mixed-use project at 1550 Bedford Avenue in Crown Heights, Brooklyn. The developer purchased the vacant parcel out of bankruptcy in August for $10.1 million. The 18-story, 173,000-square-foot building will include 250 residential units (75 designated affordable), ground-floor retail, and requires zoning amendments for residential/commercial mixed use. The site was previously controlled by Yoel Goldman, who attempted a luxury hotel project that stalled during the pandemic amid mechanic's liens exceeding $1.4 million.
stories.impact: Bankruptcy-to-development conversion in Crown Heights signals capital access for distressed Brooklyn sites and rental demand override of prior hotel strategy. Zoning flexibility (mixed-use, density bonus for affordability) indicates municipal receptivity to residential density in underutilized areas. For TCR: Crown Heights is a secondary Brooklyn submarket with improving connectivity; affordable-unit bundling improves entitlement risk. Monitor zoning approval timeline; if approved, validates multifamily-first strategy in outer Brooklyn versus hospitality.

stories.title: Anchorage Capital Advisors Takes 21K SF at 125 West 57th Street
stories.excerpt: Billionaires’ Row continues to grow as a premier office destination as a new tenant seeks to move up from Lower Manhattan. Anchorage Capital Advisors, an investment firm with more than $27.8 billion in assets under management, has signed a 10-year, 20,560-square-foot lease at Alchemy-ABR Investment Partners and Cain‘s 125 West 57th Street, the landlords announced Monday. The asking rent for the deal was not disclosed, but the average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers’ latest Manhattan office report. “We are pleased to welcome Anchorage Capital to 125 West 57th Street, joining a growing roster of best-in-class tenants at the building,” Jonathan Goldstein, CEO and co-founder of Cain, and Kenneth Horn, co-founder and partner at Alchemy-ABR Investment Partners, said in a joint statement. “125 West 57th Street was developed for firms that place a premium on quality, location and the experience they can offer their people, and Anchorage’s lease is a strong reflection of the continued demand for this type of product.” Anchorage will occupy the entire 18th and 19th floors of the 30-story office tower on 57th Street between Avenue of the Americas and Seventh Avenue. It is unclear when the firm will move, but Anchorage’s current address is listed on its website as 610 Broadway. The firm did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Tim Hay, Brian Hay and Ethan Silverstein from Cushman & Wakefield represented Anchorage in the deal, while JLL’s Mitch Konsker, Kristen Morgan, Christine Colley, Kate Roush and Dan Turkewitz represented the owners alongside Michael Werner and Elias Ayoub at Fried Frank. Representation on both sides of the deal declined CO’s requests for comment. 125 West 57th Street is also home to asset management firm Eldridge Industries and investment firm Jadin Capital. Ten Five Hospitality, which owns the Mother Wolf restaurants in Mi
bullets: Anchorage Capital Advisors leases 20,560 SF (10-year term) at 125 West 57th Street (Billionaires' Row), Manhattan.
Investment firm with $27.8B AUM relocating from Lower Manhattan; Q2 2026 Midtown office avg rent $84.99/SF.
Alchemy-ABR and Cain landlords; positioning as premier office destination amid ongoing office market churn.
stories.summary: Anchorage Capital Advisors, an investment firm managing $27.8 billion in assets under management, has signed a 10-year lease for 20,560 square feet at 125 West 57th Street on Billionaires' Row in Manhattan. The firm is relocating from Lower Manhattan. The average asking rent in Midtown during Q2 2026 was $84.99 per square foot. Alchemy-ABR Investment Partners and Cain are the landlords.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. Office leasing activity on Billionaires' Row reflects selective financial services demand concentration; does not signal broader office recovery or residential demand surge in Manhattan.

stories.title: Grubb Properties Seals $377M in Financing for Financial District Resi Tower
stories.excerpt: Fresh off the holiday weekend, a high-rise residential tower in Manhattan’s Financial District is officially inching closer to completion with some equally fresh funding. Grubb Properties just closed $377 million in construction financing for its 64-story property at 8 Carlisle Street, Commercial Observer has learned. In keeping with Grubb’s national multifamily brand, the property will be known as Link Apartments, and follows the development of Link Apartments QPN in Queens’ Long Island City, which completed construction last year. Maxim Capital Group provided the $300 million senior construction loan in the deal, while GreenBarn Investment Group, Skylight Real Estate Partners, Axonic Capital and Meadow Partners co-originated a $77 million mezzanine construction loan. The financing will facilitate the completion and lease-up of the building, which has been under construction for more than a year and is expected to top out in the next few weeks. The project’s total capitalization is around $500 million, sources familiar with the project said. Maxim Capital is plenty familiar with 8 Carlisle Street as its lending history with the asset dates back to May 2024. It provided a $67.5 million loan on the site at the time, gradually upsizing that loan to $193 million recently. Now, the firm is back with one of its largest construction loans. “It’s a huge loan for us, and one we’re very proud of,” Josh Greene, a managing director and partner at the firm, said, adding that originating larger loans such as 8 Carisle’s is part of a natural evolution for the firm. “We’ve grown substantially, and that growth has allowed us to grow into transactions like this.” The 8 Carlisle Street lending opportunity also ticks several boxes for Maxim. Roughly 70 percent of every dollar deployed by Maxim goes to repeat sponsors — like Grubb Properties — today, Greene said, and the property is also in a primary market with a residential component. “Grubb Properties is a repeat sponsor of ours, an
bullets: Grubb Properties closes $377M construction financing for Link Apartments at 8 Carlisle Street, Manhattan Financial District (64 stories).
Maxim Capital $300M senior loan, GreenBarn/Skylight/Axonic/Meadow Partners $77M mezzanine (co-originators).
Follows Link Apartments QPN in Queens LIC (completed 2025); substantial completion Q4 2028.
stories.summary: Grubb Properties has closed $377 million in construction financing for Link Apartments, a 64-story residential tower at 8 Carlisle Street in Manhattan's Financial District. Maxim Capital provided the $300 million senior construction loan, while GreenBarn Investment Group, Skylight Real Estate Partners, Axonic Capital, and Meadow Partners co-originated a $77 million mezzanine loan. The project follows Grubb's earlier Link Apartments QPN delivery in Queens' Long Island City (completed 2025). Substantial completion is expected in Q4 2028.
stories.impact: Large-scale construction debt availability ($377M mezzanine-heavy stack) for Manhattan residential signals lender appetite and market confidence in Financial District rental demand. Grubb's multi-market Link expansion (LIC + Financial District) indicates successful rental brand and capital formation for growth. For TCR: note mezzanine co-origination trend (spread risk, speed deployment); Financial District submarkets may see density competition post-2028. Monitor office-to-residential conversion velocity in Lower Manhattan; supply-side pressure on rents possible by 2028–2029.

stories.title: Abbott’s shift into reverse puts data center debate into overdrive
stories.excerpt: Texas’ typically pro-business governor did an about-face on data centers amid growing public sentiment against the resource-intensive developments.  Less than a year ago, Gov. Greg Abbott lauded Texas as “the epicenter of AI development” when Google announced a $40 billion investment in November, the Texas Tribune reported.  He changed his tune this week when he called for an outright ban on data centers in rural areas during a campaign stop in Bullard, per the outlet. The announcement built on a letter Abbott sent to the Texas Legislature in June when he called for regulating data center development in Texas. Specifically, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gov. Greg Abbott calls for rural data center ban in Texas; reverses pro-AI stance from Nov 2024.
Abbott June letter proposed regulation: annual reporting, incentive limits, closed-loop water systems for data centers.
San Marcos first Texas city to ban data centers; $50B+ data center construction nationally in 2026; HB 2559 restricts municipal bans.
stories.summary: Texas Governor Greg Abbott has shifted stance on data centers, calling for an outright ban in rural areas despite lauding Texas as the 'epicenter of AI development' less than a year ago. In June, Abbott sent a letter to the Texas Legislature proposing regulations including annual reporting, incentive limits, and closed-loop water system requirements. San Marcos became the first Texas city to attempt a data center ban. Data center construction nationwide exceeded $50 billion in 2026. State Senator Paul Bettencourt has vowed to challenge San Marcos' ban under House Bill 2559, which Abbott signed in 2025.
stories.impact: Data center regulatory tightening in Texas will constrain AI/cloud infrastructure growth-driven population migration assumptions for 2026–2027 multifamily demand forecasts in Austin, Dallas, and San Antonio metros. Water and power constraints (key anti-data-center rationale) suggest grid/utility capacity limits may also affect residential construction feasibility in high-growth corridors. For TCR: downside risk to tech-worker in-migration acceleration in Texas metros; underwriting should assume slower job-growth tailwinds and possible rent moderation. Monitor Austin/Dallas multifamily supply-demand rebalance as data center investment flattens.

stories.title: Blake Investment wins bid for $8B Tropicana Field redevelopment
stories.excerpt: A team led by Blake Investment Partners won a bid to redevelop St. Petersburg’s Historic Gas Plant District, home to Tropicana Field and one of the most sought-after development opportunities in the Southeastern United States. St. Petersburg Mayor Ken Welch selected Blake Investment Partners, led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake, to redevelop the 86-acre site, the Tampa Bay Times reported. Blake’s team includes the Related Group and hedge fund Elliott Investment Management. The firm beat out the competing finalist with “The Burg Bid,” a proposal to purchase 58 acres for $275 million and pour $8.1 billion into the project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Investment Partners wins $8.1B Tropicana Field redevelopment bid; includes Related Group and Elliott Investment Management.
86-acre Historic Gas Plant District in St. Petersburg, FL; 3,600 income-restricted units (for-rent and for-sale).
Blake's proposal: $275M land purchase, $8.1B development; two-decade project timeline; first building Woodson African American Museum.
stories.summary: Blake Investment Partners, led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake, has won the bid to redevelop St. Petersburg's Historic Gas Plant District, a high-profile 86-acre site that includes Tropicana Field. Blake's team includes the Related Group and Elliott Investment Management. The winning proposal, 'The Burg Bid,' includes purchase of 58 acres for $275 million and a total $8.1 billion development commitment. The plan includes 3,600 income-restricted housing units (both for-rent and for-sale), job creation, parks, museums, and a new home for the Woodson African American Museum. Development is expected to span approximately two decades.
stories.impact: Major mixed-use mega-project with $8.1B capital commitment and 3,600 units signals strong Southeast capital formation and income-restricted housing appetite in St. Petersburg. Affordable-unit-heavy program (anchored by public cultural amenities) indicates municipal/investor alignment on equitable development. For TCR: Tropicana Field redevelopment timing, phasing, and scale offer submarket supply/demand intelligence; early-stage ground-lease or land deals with Related may emerge. Monitor Tampa Bay metro wage growth and workforce housing demand as project begins; two-decade horizon suggests modular build-out with absorption risk assessment critical.

stories.title: Stevenson affiliate cuts unit count at proposed Glendale multifamily project
stories.excerpt: A developer is downsizing its proposed multifamily development in Glendale more than two years after the plans first surfaced.  A new application filed with the City of Glendale outlines new plans from 550 Riverdale LLC, the owner of the development site at 2748 San Fernando Road in the city’s Pacific-Edison neighborhood, Urbanize Los Angeles reported. The LLC is linked in state business records to David Stevenson, president of Glendale-based Stevenson Real Estate Services, and the real estate firm’s Glendale address. In 2024, the Robert and Stefanie Stevenson Family Trust — the property owner at the time — filed an application […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: 550 Riverdale LLC (Stevenson affiliate) downsizes proposed Glendale project from 249 to 229 units (7 stories vs. 6-story zoning).
Site at 2748 San Fernando Road; 27 affordable units; requires density-bonus entitlements to exceed zoning height.
Glendale must reach 13,425 new units by 2029 per state mandate; unit reduction suggests entitlement challenges or value engineering.
stories.summary: A developer affiliated with Glendale-based Stevenson Real Estate Services has filed revised plans to downsize a proposed multifamily project at 2748 San Fernando Road in Glendale from 249 units to 229 units. The new application calls for a seven-story, 229-unit building with 281 parking spaces across 247,000 square feet, with 27 units designated as affordable housing. The property is currently zoned for maximum six stories, requiring density-bonus entitlements. The project was initially filed in 2024.
stories.impact: Unit reduction (249 → 229) on Glendale site suggests entitlement delays or value-per-unit pressure offsetting density gains—indicates local regulatory friction or market softness. Glendale's RHNA obligation (13,425 units by 2029) creates ongoing zoning reform incentive and approvals pipeline. For TCR: Glendale remains supply-constrained relative to LA County/SoCA targets; but individual project value engineering signals tightening development economics in mid-tier LA markets. Monitor density-bonus program efficacy and competing projects for infill pace.

stories.title: LCOR lands $193M construction loan for Edgewater tower amid flurry of apartment projects
stories.excerpt: LCOR scored a $192.5 million construction loan for a 544-unit luxury apartment tower in Edgewater, as developers are excitedly pursuing multifamily projects across South Florida, despite a lingering oversupply.  New York-based LCOR started construction of a 39-story building at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Miami, according to the developer’s news release. The 1.1-acre site is a block from waterfront and Margaret Pace Park. The construction loan amounts to $354,000 per unit. Designed by ODP Architect, with interiors by Asprea Studio, the tower will include more than 40,000 square feet of amenities, over 10,000 square feet of ground-floor retail and a 448-space […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LCOR secures $192.5M construction loan for 544-unit tower at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard, Edgewater, Miami; $354K per unit.
39-story building; 40K+ SF amenities, 10K+ SF ground retail, 448-space garage; completion Q4 2028.
Site purchased 2022 for $49M; South Florida market oversupplied (18.6K units delivered 2024, outpacing absorption ~20%).
stories.summary: LCOR has secured a $192.5 million construction loan from Natixis Corporate and Investment Banking for a 544-unit luxury apartment tower at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Edgewater, Miami. The 39-story building includes over 40,000 square feet of amenities, over 10,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, and a 448-space garage. The construction loan amounts to $354,000 per unit and matures in 2029. LCOR acquired the site for $49 million in 2022 and announced the project the following year. Completion is expected in Q4 2028.
stories.impact: LCOR's $192.5M construction financing despite South Florida's documented oversupply (18.6K units delivered in 2024 vs. absorption shortfall of ~20%) signals either confident sponsor equity cushion or lender appetite for waterfront/submarket bifurcation. Edgewater location (proximity to Biscayne Boulevard, Margaret Pace Park) may justify premium thesis. For TCR: South Florida multifamily supply overhang persists; rents remain under pressure. Monitor Edgewater submarket rent trajectory closely; if rents decline despite premium positioning, broader metro rent ceiling signals cautious underwriting for 2026–2027 starts. $354K/unit loan-to-cost suggests equity injection; sponsor execution risk and lease-up speed critical.

stories.title: David Martin in talks to join Related, BH on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment
stories.excerpt: Developer David Martin is in talks to join the Related Group and BH Group on their planned redevelopment of the Hollywood Beach Resort, sources told The Real Deal.  The Pérez family’s Coconut Grove-based Related and Isaac and Liat Toledano’s Aventura-based BH Group are under contract to acquire the 4.5-acre property at 101 North Ocean Drive, as previously reported by TRD. The Chetrit Group is selling the century-old resort. A source said the property is under contract for $100 million.  Martin, CEO of Terra Group declined to comment through a spokesperson. In a statement, BH named Terra and Related as its […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: David Martin (Terra Group CEO) in talks to join Related and BH Group on 4.5-acre Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment.
Related and BH under contract for Chetrit Group's 398-unit property at 101 North Ocean Drive for ~$100M.
Terra also developing luxury condos in West Palm Beach (BH partnership, 1.6-acre site) with BH 20+ projects in South Florida.
stories.summary: David Martin, CEO of Terra Group, is negotiating to join Related Group and BH Group in redeveloping the Hollywood Beach Resort, a 4.5-acre oceanfront property at 101 North Ocean Drive. Related and BH are under contract to acquire the 398-unit century-old resort from the Chetrit Group for approximately $100 million. Terra is also partnering with BH on a luxury condo development at 2085 North Flagler Drive in West Palm Beach. BH Group has over 20 projects with Related across South Florida.
stories.impact: Oceanfront resort-to-residential conversion signals capital-intensive land banking and repositioning in South Florida; Related-BH-Terra partnership density indicates coordinated South Florida multifamily/mixed-use strategy across waterfront submarkets. Terra's involvement adds condo development expertise alongside Related rental; watch for mixed-tenure product strategy. For TCR: monitor deal closure timing (vested development rights pending); Hollywood Beach submarket supply and absorption impact material to South Florida metro thesis. Waterfront infill commanding premium capital; basis compression if resort conversion timelines slip.
